children.name: Cuon
children.description: Cuon — a genus of dogs & wolves in the family Canidae.

children.name: Cuon alpinus
children.description: The dhole is a canid native to South, East and Southeast Asia. The sole member of the genus Cuon, it is anatomically distinguished from members of the genus Canis in several aspects: its skull is convex rather than concave in profile, it lacks a third lower molar, and the upper molars possess only a single cusp as opposed to between two and four. During the Pleistocene, the dhole ranged throughout Asia, with its range also extending into Europe but became restricted to its historical range 12,000–18,000 years ago.

children.name: Lycalopex sechurae
children.description: The Sechuran fox, also called the Peruvian desert fox or the Sechuran zorro, is a small South American species of canid closely related to other South American "false" foxes or zorro. It gets its name for being found in the Sechura Desert in northwestern Peru.

children.name: Vulpes lagopus
children.description: The Arctic fox, also known as the white fox, polar fox, or snow fox, is a small species of fox native to the Arctic regions of the Northern Hemisphere and common throughout the Arctic tundra. It is well adapted to living in cold environments, and is known for its thick, warm fur that can be used as camouflage against snow in the winter. It has a large and fluffy tail.

children.name: Vulpes qiuzhudingi
children.description: Vulpes qiuzhudingi (also known as Tibetan Fox or Pliocene fox) is an extinct species of fox that lived during the Neogene period in the Himalayas.

children.name: Vulpes riffautae
children.description: Vulpes riffautae is an extinct species of fox from the late Miocene of Chad (approximately 7 ma).

children.name: Aelurodon asthenostylus
children.description: Aelurodon is an extinct canid genus of the subfamily Borophaginae which lived from the Barstovian land mammal age of the middle Miocene to the late Miocene epoch. Aelurodon existed for approximately 10.7 million years.

children.name: Canis wheelerianus

children.name: Xenocyon lycaonoides
children.description: Xenocyon is an extinct group of canids, either considered a distinct genus or a subgenus of Canis. The group includes Canis (Xenocyon) africanus, Canis (Xenocyon) antonii and Canis (Xenocyon) falconeri that gave rise to Canis (Xenocyon) lycanoides. The hypercarnivorous Xenocyon is thought to be closely related and possibly ancestral to modern dhole and the African wild dog, as well as the insular Sardinian dhole.
